interest: Equity advocates, reform movements, and comprehensive-school proponents invoke the cross-national evidence to justify structural abolition of early tracking, while opponents (conservatives, employers) attack the Hungary-specific mechanism gap to resist it.
value: Equity (reducing SES-outcome inequality) versus excellence/talent-pipeline preservation, with an implicit tension between evidentiary rigor and reform ambition.
fear: Equity proponents fear that early selection permanently entrenches disadvantage for Roma and low-SES pupils; opponents fear the equity gain is speculative because the causal peer-effect mechanism is unproven in Hungary and sorting will simply relocate.
affected: employers relying on the talent pipeline
assumption: The claim assumes the cross-national delayed-tracking effect transfers to Hungary despite the acknowledged weakness of the Hungary-specific peer-effect mechanism, and that mean performance is preserved under Hungarian conditions.
empirical_uncertainty: The cross-national fact (Hanushek & Woessmann 2006: delayed tracking reduces SES-outcome inequality without harming mean performance) is well-evidenced, but the Hungary-specific transmission mechanism (peer effects) is explicitly tagged weak/contested by the equity expert, making it a genuine causal-mechanism uncertainty rather than settled.
